steps.title: Identify the structure
steps.content: Before the blank: Kizomba.has become an international [BLANK] — an independent clause ends at the blank. After the blank: In 2022, the Indian dance duo.defeated performers. — a second complete independent clause. Two ICs cannot be joined by a comma alone.

steps.title: Apply the rule
steps.content:
Identify the structure on each side: left IC = "Kizomba.has become an international phenomenon"; right IC = "In 2022, the Indian dance duo.defeated performers.". Two ICs require a period, semicolon, or comma + coordinating conjunction.
  • "phenomenon and in": ".international phenomenon and in 2022, the Indian dance duo." — "and" joins the two ICs but without a comma before it, creating a run-on; also warps the meaning of "in 2022." ✗
  • "phenomenon. In": ".international phenomenon. In 2022, the Indian dance duo." — period correctly ends the first IC; "In 2022" begins a new sentence cleanly. ✓
  • "phenomenon, in": ".international phenomenon, in 2022, the Indian dance duo." — comma between two ICs is a comma splice. ✗
  • "phenomenon in": ".international phenomenon in 2022, the Indian dance duo." — no punctuation between ICs creates a run-on sentence. ✗
That matches the correct answer.
